questions.prompt: What is a primary role of an operating-system kernel?

answers.text: Manage hardware resources and provide protected services to programs.

feedback.legit: Manage hardware resources and provide protected services to programs. Marking it Legit was correct.
feedback.sus: Manage hardware resources and provide protected services to programs. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: Compilation is performed by development tools, not the kernel's core runtime role.

feedback.legit: Compilation is performed by development tools, not the kernel's core runtime role. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Compilation is performed by development tools, not the kernel's core runtime role. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: System calls provide controlled entry to kernel services.

feedback.legit: System calls provide controlled entry to kernel services. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: System calls provide controlled entry to kernel services. This one should stay marked Legit.

questions.prompt: What does virtual memory give a process?

answers.text: Direct unrestricted access to all physical memory.

feedback.legit: Address translation and protection isolate process memory. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Address translation and protection isolate process memory. You spotted the misleading part.

answers.text: Different processes can use the same virtual address for different physical storage.

feedback.legit: Different processes can use the same virtual address for different physical storage. The source agrees with your Legit vote.
feedback.sus: Different processes can use the same virtual address for different physical storage. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

answers.text: Its own virtual address space mapped to physical memory and other backing.

feedback.legit: Its own virtual address space mapped to physical memory and other backing. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Its own virtual address space mapped to physical memory and other backing. This one should stay marked Legit.

questions.prompt: What happens in a context switch?

answers.text: A context switch can occur only between different processes, never between threads.

feedback.legit: The scheduler can switch between threads, including threads in the same process. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: The scheduler can switch between threads, including threads in the same process. Your vote matches the correction.

answers.text: The system saves one execution context and restores another.

feedback.legit: The system saves one execution context and restores another. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: The system saves one execution context and restores another. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: The CPU permanently erases the previous process.

feedback.legit: The previous task can resume from its saved state. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: The previous task can resume from its saved state. You were right not to trust that reply.

questions.prompt: Does every page fault indicate a program bug?

answers.text: Many page faults are handled normally by loading or mapping a page.

feedback.legit: Many page faults are handled normally by loading or mapping a page. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Many page faults are handled normally by loading or mapping a page. This is a Legit statement, not a trap.

answers.text: An invalid access can produce a fault the OS cannot resolve for the process.

feedback.legit: An invalid access can produce a fault the OS cannot resolve for the process. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: An invalid access can produce a fault the OS cannot resolve for the process. The rule confirms the reply instead of refuting it.

answers.text: Demand paging relies on recoverable page faults.

feedback.legit: Demand paging relies on recoverable page faults. Legit was the accurate read.
feedback.sus: Demand paging relies on recoverable page faults. The evidence supports the original reply.

questions.prompt: How do threads within one process normally relate?

answers.text: Each thread has a completely separate copy of all process memory.

feedback.legit: Threads share memory but have separate stacks and execution state. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Threads share memory but have separate stacks and execution state. Sus was the correct call here.

answers.text: Shared memory makes communication cheap but introduces synchronization risks.

feedback.legit: Shared memory makes communication cheap but introduces synchronization risks. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: Shared memory makes communication cheap but introduces synchronization risks. There was no false claim to flag here.

answers.text: They share the process address space and resources.

feedback.legit: They share the process address space and resources. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: They share the process address space and resources. This one was safe to mark Legit.

questions.prompt: How does a counting semaphore differ from a mutex?

answers.text: Declaring a semaphore automatically protects shared data even when code ignores its protocol.

feedback.legit: Synchronization works only when all relevant accesses follow the semaphore protocol correctly. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: Synchronization works only when all relevant accesses follow the semaphore protocol correctly. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: A semaphore can represent multiple available permits.

feedback.legit: A semaphore can represent multiple available permits. That is a solid Legit pick.
feedback.sus: A semaphore can represent multiple available permits. This one should stay marked Legit.

answers.text: A semaphore always has one owner that must release it.

feedback.legit: Ownership is a mutex concept; semaphore use need not enforce the same owner. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Ownership is a mutex concept; semaphore use need not enforce the same owner. Your vote matches the correction.

questions.prompt: What makes a race condition possible?

answers.text: The result depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent accesses.

feedback.legit: The result depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent accesses. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: The result depends on uncontrolled timing of concurrent accesses. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: Merely having two functions in the same source file.

feedback.legit: Concurrency and shared or interacting state are required for a timing race. The reply changes an important technical fact.
feedback.sus: Concurrency and shared or interacting state are required for a timing race. Good read: the original claim was flawed.

answers.text: Synchronization can establish a defined order or atomic access.

feedback.legit: Synchronization can establish a defined order or atomic access. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Synchronization can establish a defined order or atomic access. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: Which conditions are associated with classic resource deadlock?

answers.text: High CPU utilization alone.

feedback.legit: Load can be high without any circular resource dependency. The original reply is what needed flagging.
feedback.sus: Load can be high without any circular resource dependency. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: Breaking one necessary condition can prevent this form of deadlock.

feedback.legit: Breaking one necessary condition can prevent this form of deadlock. That makes your Legit call correct.
feedback.sus: Breaking one necessary condition can prevent this form of deadlock. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait.

feedback.legit: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. This supports the Legit label you chose.
feedback.sus: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. There was no false claim to flag here.

questions.prompt: What tradeoff does a CPU scheduler manage?

answers.text: Giving a task lower priority guarantees that it will still run before starvation is possible.

feedback.legit: Priority scheduling needs an explicit fairness or aging policy to prevent starvation. The source contradicts the original reply.
feedback.sus: Priority scheduling needs an explicit fairness or aging policy to prevent starvation. The Sus label fits the misconception.

answers.text: Responsiveness, throughput, fairness, and overhead.

feedback.legit: Responsiveness, throughput, fairness, and overhead. You were right to trust this reply.
feedback.sus: Responsiveness, throughput, fairness, and overhead. The reply belongs under Legit.

answers.text: It can maximize every metric simultaneously for every workload.

feedback.legit: Scheduling goals conflict and depend on workload. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: Scheduling goals conflict and depend on workload. Your judgment caught the technical error.

questions.prompt: What is copy-on-write after process creation?

answers.text: Processes initially share pages until one attempts to modify a shared page.

feedback.legit: Processes initially share pages until one attempts to modify a shared page. The evidence backs your Legit choice.
feedback.sus: Processes initially share pages until one attempts to modify a shared page. The claim is supported rather than suspicious.

answers.text: Every page is copied before the child can run.

feedback.legit: Copy-on-write postpones copies and avoids copying untouched pages. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Copy-on-write postpones copies and avoids copying untouched pages. That makes your Sus choice accurate.

answers.text: A write fault can trigger creation of a private copy.

feedback.legit: A write fault can trigger creation of a private copy. You classified that correctly as Legit.
feedback.sus: A write fault can trigger creation of a private copy. The original wording holds up.

answers.text: A CPU executes instructions too quickly for memory.

feedback.legit: Thrashing comes from an insufficient working set and heavy paging. The error puts the original reply under Sus.
feedback.sus: Thrashing comes from an insufficient working set and heavy paging. That was correctly classified as Sus.

answers.text: Reducing active working sets or adding memory can help.

feedback.legit: Reducing active working sets or adding memory can help. Your Legit vote was right.
feedback.sus: Reducing active working sets or adding memory can help. This was accurate as written.

answers.text: The system spends excessive time moving pages instead of doing useful work.

feedback.legit: The system spends excessive time moving pages instead of doing useful work. Your Legit judgment holds up.
feedback.sus: The system spends excessive time moving pages instead of doing useful work. This reply deserved a Legit vote.

questions.prompt: Why does an operating system cache file data in memory?

answers.text: The operating-system page cache is the same hardware structure as a CPU cache.

feedback.legit: The page cache is managed by the kernel for file data; CPU caches are hardware caches for memory accesses. The original reply should be marked Sus.
feedback.sus: The page cache is managed by the kernel for file data; CPU caches are hardware caches for memory accesses. You were right not to trust that reply.

answers.text: Reading file data from the page cache always performs a new disk read.

feedback.legit: A cache hit can satisfy the read from memory without accessing the storage device again. The Legit label misses the flaw here.
feedback.sus: A cache hit can satisfy the read from memory without accessing the storage device again. The evidence supports your Sus vote.

answers.text: Cached writes are guaranteed durable immediately.

feedback.legit: Durability requires the data to reach the required stable storage boundary. The original statement is materially misleading.
feedback.sus: Durability requires the data to reach the required stable storage boundary. That was correctly classified as Sus.
